# Carbon Credits, Canopy Loss: The Congo Forest Project That Couldn't Beat the Baseline

**By [Environmental Correspondent], The Guardian**

A flagship carbon offsetting project in the Democratic Republic of the Congo, marketed as a bulwark against deforestation in one of the world's most critical forest ecosystems, has lost nearly 20,000 hectares of tree cover since its launch — and satellite analysis shows it performed no better at protecting forest than comparable unprotected areas nearby.

The finding strikes at the heart of the voluntary carbon market's foundational promise: that credits represent real, additional forest protection that would not have happened otherwise.

Satellite data analyzed by CarbonVerifier reveals that the Mai Ndombe REDD+ Project (VCS934), a 299,640-hectare concession registered with Verra in the Democratic Republic of the Congo, shed 19,820 hectares of forest in the years following its February 2011 start date — nearly three times the 6,741 hectares lost in the preceding decade. Among the companies that have purchased credits from the project are Salesforce and Engie.

---

**A Decade of Mounting Loss**

The satellite record tells a story of early promise followed by accelerating failure.

In the years immediately before the project launched, annual forest loss inside the boundary was running at a few hundred hectares per year: 836 hectares in 2008, 746 in 2009, before spiking to 1,927 hectares in 2010, the final full year before registration. Those numbers, troubling in themselves, would come to look modest.

In 2013 — just two years into the project's operational life — the boundary recorded a catastrophic single-year loss of 4,257.6 hectares, according to satellite data analyzed by CarbonVerifier using the Hansen Global Forest Change dataset. That figure is more than six times the loss recorded in the project's first year and represents the worst single year in the entire 23-year satellite record for this site.

The years that followed brought no sustained recovery. Loss ran at 1,932 hectares in 2014, dipped briefly, then surged again: 2,264 hectares in 2018, 1,957 in 2020, and a new post-project record of 2,450.7 hectares in 2023 — the most recent year for which data are available. In total, forest loss after the project's start date accounts for 7.1% of the area's year-2000 forest cover of 280,072 hectares.

---

**The Additionality Question**

Under the rules of REDD+ carbon accounting, credits are only supposed to be issued for forest protection that is *additional* — meaning it would not have occurred without the project's intervention. The satellite comparison with nearby unprotected forest areas demolishes that claim.

Satellite data analyzed by CarbonVerifier compared the project's post-start loss rate against five control areas in the same latitude band — unprotected forest tracts of broadly similar character. The project's loss rate of 7.08% was actually *higher* than the mean loss rate across the control areas, which stood at 5.61%. The difference of 1.47 percentage points runs in the wrong direction: the supposedly protected project forest fared worse than the unprotected comparison forest.

The control areas themselves showed wide variation — from a loss rate of just 0.64% in one zone to 14.75% in another — but averaged together, they suggest that simply doing nothing in comparable nearby forest would have produced better outcomes than the credited intervention. CarbonVerifier's verdict: no additionality demonstrated.

---

**What This Means for the Market**

The Mai Ndombe project is not an obscure footnote. It is a registered, verified Verra project that has attracted corporate buyers including Salesforce and Engie — household names that have cited nature-based offsets as part of their climate commitments.

If the satellite record is accurate, every tonne of carbon credited by this project against a deforestation baseline that the project then failed to beat represents a credit with no corresponding forest protection in the real world. The voluntary carbon market's credibility depends on the premise that verification catches exactly this kind of outcome. Here, the trees tell a different story than the registry.
